title: Symplectic Excision and Distance Rigidity
speaker: Yoel Groman
affiliation: Hebrew University of Jerusalem Einstein Institute of Mathematics
abstract:
A symplectic manifold 
M
 is called tame at infinity if it admits a compatible almost complex structure such that the corresponding Riemannian metric is complete and geometrically bounded. Some such condition is necessary to confine 
J
-holomorphic curves of finite symplectic area. In fact, the strict geometric boundedness condition can be relaxed to a weakly contractible condition that still allows for the same confinement.
Because there is no distinguished such almost complex structure, we ask: Are there geometric features common to all of them? We investigate this through the lens of distances between subsets of 
M
. A non-quantitative version of the same question is: does 
M
 remain tame upon excising a subset? We find rigidity phenomena when excising symplectic hypersurfaces, which contrast with the flexibility that often occurs when the excised set is coisotropic.

proof_stages.label: Tameness, bounded geometry, and monotonicity
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines tameness at infinity by the existence of a complete compatible almost complex structure with curvature and injectivity-radius bounds, and explains that Sikorav-type monotonicity then confines finite-energy holomorphic curves.

proof_stages.label: Excision problem and motivating landscape
proof_stages.summary: The speaker asks when removing a subset preserves tameness, contrasting volume-based obstructions with coisotropic-style excision phenomena and explaining the resulting Floer-theoretic locality motivation.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity theorems and distance formulation
proof_stages.summary: Two rigidity results are stated—complements of properly embedded symplectic hypersurfaces and proper convex cotangent preimages are not tame—and then refined into bounds on how far subsets can be separated by uniformly geometrically bounded compatible metrics.

proof_stages.label: Cylinder model for the holomorphic-disk mechanism
proof_stages.summary: In the two-dimensional cylinder case, an elementary injectivity-radius estimate is recast as a holomorphic-disk argument after symplectic cutting, suggesting the proof strategy for higher dimensions.

proof_stages.label: Localization inside a Delzant polytope
proof_stages.summary: For the convex cotangent theorem, a metric ball of radius below the desired distance is projected into the convex set and enclosed in a compact Delzant polytope with a prescribed primitive normal, allowing passage to a compact toric variety while keeping the original almost complex structure on the ball.

proof_stages.label: Bounded-area toric holomorphic disk
proof_stages.summary: Toric holomorphic-curve/Fukaya-algebra input gives, for any extension of the almost complex structure, a nontrivial holomorphic disk with boundary on the chosen Lagrangian fiber and area bounded by the flux associated to the selected primitive vector.

proof_stages.label: Monotonicity converts area to distance rigidity
proof_stages.summary: Applying local monotonicity to disjoint balls along the holomorphic disk bounds its diameter by its area and the geometric-bound constant, yielding the promised quantitative distance estimate and hence non-tameness of proper convex cotangent preimages.

proof_stages.label: Meridian-disk proof for hypersurface rigidity
proof_stages.summary: For a symplectic hypersurface, a Darboux-chart product torus carries a meridian disk linking the hypersurface, and persistence of a minimal-area Fukaya contribution supplies disks for arbitrary compatible structures, bounding the distance to the hypersurface.

examples.name: Punctured Riemann-surface cusp metrics
examples.note: Complete constant-curvature cusp metrics on punctured surfaces show that completeness and curvature bounds alone do not imply tameness because the injectivity radius can collapse.
examples.engagement_reason: he invokes punctured Riemann-surface cusp metrics as complete constant-curvature metrics whose injectivity radius goes to zero, with no further analysis

passes.reason: Cusp metrics on punctured Riemann surfaces have a collapsed thin end and are used to show completeness and curvature bounds do not give full geometric boundedness.

examples.name: Characteristic ray excision in a cylinder
examples.note: A ray, illustrated by removing a ray from an infinite cylinder, has a complement symplectomorphic to the original manifold and so demonstrates an excisable coisotropic-type set.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the ray-excision idea on an infinite cylinder and explains that removing a characteristic ray or family can be slid off to recover a symplectomorphic complement

examples.name: Focus-focus anti-surgery yielding T^*T^2
examples.note: A focus-focus Lagrangian torus fibration, also realized by an affine variety with an Auroux fibration, has a monodromy-invariant ray lifting to a Lagrangian disk whose removal gives the cotangent bundle of the two-torus, motivating the locality viewpoint.
examples.engagement_reason: he traces the focus-focus fibration, lifts a monodromy-invariant ray to a Lagrangian disk, and identifies the complement as T^*T^2

passes.reason: The focus-focus setup contains a pinched singular torus but is presented as a successful anti-surgery/excision yielding T^*T^2.
